name: aks
context: Create AKS clusters, fetch kubeconfig, and manage node pools with Azure CLI before handing off to Kubernetes tools
info: Azure Kubernetes Service is Azure's managed Kubernetes control plane. Agents use this skill to create a cluster, fetch credentials into kubeconfig, inspect cluster state, and add node pools for workload isolation or capacity changes. The core execution surface is `az aks` and `az aks nodepool`, after which control typically hands off to [[kubernetes/kubectl]], [[kubernetes/deployments]], or [[kubernetes/helm]].
triggers: add a node pool to aks
connect kubectl to azure kubernetes service
scale or isolate workloads with aks node pools
body:
# Azure Kubernetes Service

AKS is the Azure-managed control plane for Kubernetes. The Azure part of the job is cluster provisioning, identity, networking defaults, and kubeconfig retrieval. The Kubernetes part of the job starts after credentials land on disk.

~~~bash
# Create a cluster
az aks create \
  --resource-group rg-dojo-aks \
  --name dojo-aks-prod \
  --location eastus \
  --node-count 3 \
  --node-vm-size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Merge credentials into kubeconfig
az aks get-credentials --resource-group rg-dojo-aks --name dojo-aks-prod
kubectl get nodes

# Add a specialized node pool
az aks nodepool add \
  --resource-group rg-dojo-aks \
  --cluster-name dojo-aks-prod \
  --name gpu \
  --node-count 1 \
  --node-vm-size Standard_NC4as_T4_v3
~~~

Do not treat the Azure-managed node resource group like a normal hand-edited group. Microsoft's AKS troubleshooting guidance explicitly shows autoscaler failures caused by modified or missing node resource group tags. Let AKS manage that infrastructure boundary unless you have a documented product-specific reason not to.

After `az aks get-credentials`, switch immediately to [[kubernetes/kubectl]] and the generic Kubernetes nodes in this graph.

sections.title: Create the cluster
sections.body: `az aks create` is the provisioning step. Supply the resource group, cluster name, region, node count, and node VM size at minimum. Add extra flags such as `--attach-acr`, `--kubernetes-version`, or `--enable-azure-monitor-metrics` only when the deployment plan requires them.

sections.title: Fetch credentials for kubectl
sections.body: `az aks get-credentials --resource-group ... --name ...` merges the cluster credentials into kubeconfig so `kubectl` can operate on the cluster. Use `--overwrite-existing` when the local kubeconfig entry may already exist and should be refreshed deterministically.

sections.title: Node pools
sections.body: `az aks nodepool add` creates an additional node pool for workload isolation, cost segmentation, or special hardware. After creation, use the generic Kubernetes nodes to apply labels, taints, Helm releases, or workload manifests against the cluster.

sections.title: Managed node resource group caution
sections.body: AKS owns a separate node resource group for infrastructure such as scale sets and load balancer artifacts. Microsoft's troubleshooting docs show that modifying or deleting Azure-managed tags in that group can break autoscaling behavior. Treat that group as AKS-owned state rather than a free-form customization target.

related.note: AKS is the Azure-managed way to obtain a Kubernetes cluster for generic kubectl workflows
Helm is a common next step after the cluster is provisioned
AKS workloads normally depend on container images built elsewhere in the graph

scripts.name: Manage AKS
scripts.description: Create an AKS cluster, fetch credentials, inspect it, or add node pools

AZURE_AKS_ACTION.description: One of cluster-create, get-credentials, nodepool-add, or show

AZURE_AKS_NODE_COUNT.description: Initial node count for cluster create or node pool add

AZURE_AKS_NODE_VM_SIZE.description: VM size for node pool creation

AZURE_AKS_ATTACH_ACR.description: Optional ACR name or resource ID to attach during cluster create

AZURE_AKS_NODEPOOL_NAME.description: Additional node pool name for nodepool-add

AZURE_AKS_OVERWRITE_EXISTING.description: Set true to pass --overwrite-existing to get-credentials

AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID.description: Subscription ID or name to activate before the command

nodeCount.description: Node count for the default pool or a new node pool

id.description: ARM resource ID for the cluster

depends.reason: Cluster lifecycle operations require an authenticated Azure principal
AKS clusters are created inside a resource group
After get-credentials, cluster operations move to kubectl
The executable wrapper is implemented in Bash

author: dojo-community
